title: The Sense of Kehai — What AI Taught Me About Presence
excerpt: There's a Japanese word — 気配 (kehai) — the sense of something before it appears. A samurai feels the enemy before seeing him. I didn't expect to find that feeling in an AI-generated image.
body: There's a Japanese word — 気配 (kehai) — the sense of something before it appears. A rustling before the wind. A shadow before the light shifts. You feel it in the body before the mind names it.

I didn't expect to find kehai in an AI-generated image. But one afternoon, while reviewing the samurai assets I'd generated for bokusai, one stopped me. The warrior wasn't mid-swing. He was still. Seated. The blade not drawn, just resting beside him.

Most people look at a samurai and expect violence. Action. The raised sword. But the real story of bushido isn't about fighting — it's about the space before the fight. The stillness that contains everything that might happen.

That's what kehai is. That image had it. I'm still not entirely sure why. Maybe it was the way the light fell through the shoji screens. Maybe it was the angle — the warrior looking slightly off-frame, as if he had heard something we couldn't.

When I create these images, I'm not just typing prompts. I'm trying to find that feeling. The Japanese idea of 間 (ma) — negative space, the pause between notes, the gap between breaths — is just as important as what's in the frame.
AI can generate beauty. It can generate chaos and noise. But kehai — that particular quality of presence — that requires intention. That requires knowing what to leave out.
I think that's what I'm actually selling at bokusai. Not just images. The feeling that something important is about to happen.

title: Why 9:16 Is the New Haiku
excerpt: A haiku is 17 syllables. A Reels vertical is 9:16. Both are extreme constraints. Both force you to find the one thing that matters. I didn't plan bokusai to be about poetry — but here we are.
body: A haiku is 17 syllables. A vertical Reels frame is 9:16 — narrow, tall, unforgiving. Both are extreme constraints. Both force a single question: what is the one thing that needs to be here?
Matsuo Bashō didn't write haiku because he lacked space. He chose the constraint. The limitation wasn't a problem — it was the point. When you only have 17 syllables, every word becomes load-bearing.

When I decided to make all bokusai images 9:16 vertical, it wasn't just a technical choice for social media. It changed how I thought about composition. You can't fit a wide mountain panorama. You have to choose: is this image about the summit, or the base, or the sky above?

With Mount Fuji, I almost always choose the sky. Not because the summit isn't beautiful — it is — but because the sky above Fuji tells you something the mountain alone can't. It tells you how small the mountain is. How infinite the world is beyond it.
That's a haiku thought. The contrast. The thing the image is actually about hiding just at the edge of what's visible.

Video creators who use bokusai footage in their Shorts and Reels understand this instinctively. They don't need me to explain that a vertical frame of Mount Fuji at dawn isn't just a nature shot — it's a mood, a question, a beginning.
The constraint of 9:16 is what makes that possible. Just like the constraint of 17 syllables made Bashō's frogs jump across centuries.

title: What Makes Edo-Period Light Different
excerpt: Before electricity, Japanese interior light came from candles and paper screens. It was warm, directional, and it moved. That quality of light — not uniform, not bright — is what I try to recreate in every Edo image.
body: Before electricity, the only interior light in Japan came from andon lamps — oil candles inside paper frames — and from the diffused glow through shoji screens. It was warm, directional, and it moved. The flame moved. The shadows moved. The light was alive in a way that modern LED panels can't replicate.

When I generate Edo-period images for bokusai, I'm always hunting for that quality of light. Not the uniform brightness of a studio shot. Not the flat illumination of a daylight scene. I want the light that makes one side of a face visible and leaves the other in shadow.

There's a word in Japanese aesthetics: 陰翳 (in'ei) — shadow, or more precisely, the beauty that exists within shadow. The writer Jun'ichirō Tanizaki wrote a whole essay about it in 1933. He argued that Japanese aesthetics had always been built on the appreciation of low light, of darkness, of the way objects reveal themselves slowly rather than all at once.
An LED-lit room shows you everything immediately. A candle-lit room makes you wait. Makes you look closer. Makes you uncertain about what you're seeing.

That uncertainty — that productive ambiguity — is what I try to build into every Edo-era image I create. The gold of a kimono that might be shadow. The face that turns away just slightly. The paper screen that glows from behind without revealing what cast the light.
When you use these images in your YouTube videos, I hope that quality travels. That the viewer leans in slightly. That they feel like they're seeing something they're not quite supposed to see.
